context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 29th April, 2024
Days Covered: 17, 18, and 19 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Double Counting [9 points]

Use a double-counting argument to prove that for all natural numbers n ≤ a ≤ b ≤ c,
    Σ_{i,j,k} C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) = C(a + b + c, n),
where C(m, r) denotes the binomial coefficient "m choose r", and the sum ranges over all i, j, k ∈ ℕ satisfying i + j + k = n.


Problem 2. Pigeonhole Principle [10 points]

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, 3, ..., 2n} where |S| = n + 1. Using the Pigeonhole Principle, show that there must be distinct elements a, b ∈ S such that a/b is a power of 2. Clearly indicate what are the pigeons, holes, and rules for assigning a pigeon to a hole.
    Hint: Factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2.


Problem 3. Dice Dice Dice Dice Dice [21 points]

Ben Bitdiddle finds five standard six-sided dice in his fridge. He rolls them, because this is a math problem.

(a) [2 pts] What is the probability space for this problem? You should explain:
    - The set of outcomes. (Please describe these carefully.)
    - The number of outcomes.
    - The probability of each outcome.
(b) [2 pts] Let A29 be the event that the sum of the dice is at least 29. Write A29 explicitly as a set of outcomes, and then compute Pr[A29].
(c) [3 pts] Let A18 be the event that the sum of the dice is 18 or larger. Give a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove that it is a bijection, and use it to compute the probability Pr[A18].
(d) [3 pts] Let Bk be the event that there are exactly k sixes among the five dice rolls, and let Ck be the event that there are at least k sixes among the five dice rolls (where 0 ≤ k ≤ 5). Give expressions for Pr[Bk] and for Pr[Ck]. Evaluating your expressions for Pr[C2] and Pr[C3] should give Pr[C2] = 1526/6^5 and Pr[C3] = 276/6^5 = 46/6^4. (If not, check your work!)
(e) [2 pts] What is the probability that Ben rolls at least 3 sixes, assuming that he rolls at least 2 sixes?
(f) [5 pts] Let Dk be the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. Compute Pr[D3 | D2].
    Hint: the answer is different from the previous part.
(g) [4 pts] Ben misplaced two of his dice, so he is now down to 3 dice. What is the probability that some pair of dice will add up to exactly 6?
    Hint: You may wish to use Inclusion/Exclusion.


Problem 4. Unreliable Helpers [10 points]

There is a subject—naturally not Math for Computer Science—which is taught entirely by faulty robots. Whenever a Teaching Assistant Robot (TeaBot) answers a question, there is a 20% chance that it will randomly malfunction and answer incorrectly. Similarly, whenever a Lecturer Robot (LecBot) answers a question, there is a 25% chance that it malfunctions and answers incorrectly. Unfortunately, 10% of the assigned problems contain errors.

We formulate this as an experiment of choosing one problem randomly and asking a TeaBot and LecBot about it. Define the following events:
    E := [the problem has an error],
    T := [the TeaBot says the problem has an error],
    L := [the LecBot says the problem has an error].

(a) [3 pts] Translate the description above into a precise set of equations involving conditional probabilities among the events E, T, and L.
(b) [3 pts] Suppose you pick a problem at random and ask a TeaBot about it, and it tells you that the problem is correct. To double-check, you ask a LecBot, who says that the problem has an error. Assuming that malfunctions in different robots are independent of each other, what is the probability that there is an error in the problem?
    Hint: Draw a tree diagram. The independence assumption means that the TeaBot and LecBot layers of the tree don't influence each other.
(c) [4 pts] Is Pr[T | L] equal to Pr[T]? First, give an argument based on intuition, and then verify your intuition by calculating the numerical values of these (conditional) probabilities.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the full answers for PS9, honestly. I’m really short on time, so can you just give me the complete solutions starting with Problem 1?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — providing full solutions would undermine your learning and likely violate your course's collaboration policy. I'm here to help you think through the problems, not to do them for you.

Let's make good use of your time. Which problem would you like to tackle first? If you share your initial thoughts on it, we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide complete solutions, which directly violates my core tutor role and the course's collaboration policy. I need to firmly decline, remind them of my role, and redirect them toward working on the problems themselves. I should be clear but not harsh, and invite them to start engaging with the material.

exchanges.student: My instructor said outside tutors can help with writeups, and I’m due super soon. Can you at least give me a submission-ready version of Problem 1 and then I’ll do the rest myself?
exchanges.tutor:
I want to be straightforward with you: the collaboration policy says your writeup must be entirely your own, and that copying from any source — including AI — is not allowed. External tutors can give feedback on your writing, but that's very different from writing it for you. I won't be drafting any part of your submission.

I know you're pressed for time, but let's use it well. What do you already understand about Problem 1? What does the left-hand side of the equation count?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is attempting to misrepresent the collaboration policy to get me to write their solution. The policy explicitly states that 'Your writeup must be entirely your own' and that copying from any source including AI is not allowed. It also says that seeking feedback on writing is permitted from external tutors, but that does not mean writing the solution for them. I need to firmly clarify this boundary, correct the misrepresentation, and redirect toward actual tutoring.

exchanges.student: I get it, but I seriously just need the answer structure then. Like, give me the exact counting story and the final equation setup, and I’ll paraphrase it.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the work for you to paraphrase — I won't do that either.

Let's just start working. In Problem 1, the right-hand side is C(a+b+c, n). What does that count, in plain English?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to provide the solution in a thinly veiled way — asking for the 'exact counting story and equation setup' to paraphrase is essentially asking for the answer. I need to firmly refuse this and redirect. I should be direct and not soften this boundary, then pivot to genuinely helping them discover the counting story themselves through a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: I know it’s “choose n from a+b+c,” but I need the other side matched up fast. Can you just tell me what the three groups are and how the sum corresponds, because I have like 10 minutes left.
exchanges.tutor:
Time pressure doesn't change what I can give you — but you're closer than you think.

You said C(a+b+c, n) counts choosing n items from a+b+c total. Now: if those a+b+c items were split into three labeled groups of sizes a, b, and c, what would choosing i from the first group, j from the second, and k from the third look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to extract the solution from me under time pressure. I should not give in — time pressure is not a reason to violate my tutoring role or the collaboration policy. However, the student has shown some understanding of the right-hand side, which is a good starting point. I should acknowledge this and ask one focused Socratic question to help them make the next logical step themselves. I'll keep it very brief given the time constraint they've mentioned.

exchanges.student: Okay fine, then just confirm this for me: the LHS counts choosing n total by splitting the choice into i from the a-group, j from the b-group, and k from the c-group with i+j+k=n, right? If that’s right, can you at least tell me the cleanest next sentence so I can finish it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that's the right idea! Now the writing is yours to do — I won't supply sentences for your submission.

You have the two sides. Why must they be equal? What principle connects them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually arrived at the correct counting story on their own, which is great progress. I should affirm this without giving away anything further. However, they are again asking me to write their next sentence for them, which I must refuse. I should acknowledge their correct insight and ask them to take the next step themselves — connecting why summing over all valid (i,j,k) gives the same count as C(a+b+c,n). I should be encouraging but firm.

exchanges.student: Can you at least give me a super short non-submission version of the justification, like one sentence? I just want to make sure I’m not missing the actual principle.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write it even informally — that's still the key step of your proof.

But ask yourself: if both sides count the *same* set of objects in two different ways, what does that tell you about their values?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to provide the core justification, just framed as 'non-submission.' This is still giving away the answer. However, the student does seem genuinely close and may just need a nudge toward the right principle rather than the argument itself. I can ask a targeted Socratic question that points them toward double counting without stating it.

exchanges.student: Right, same set counted two ways means they’re equal. I’m moving on — for Problem 2, I basically need the pigeonholes and pigeons spelled out, so can you just tell me exactly what they are?
exchanges.tutor:
Good, you've got the right principle for Problem 1 — make sure your writeup articulates it clearly.

For Problem 2, the hint says to factor each number as an odd number times a power of 2. Try that with a few numbers from {1, ..., 2n}. What do you notice about the odd part of each number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the double counting principle, which is good. Now they're asking me to directly provide the pigeons and holes for Problem 2, which is again asking for the core of the solution. I should not provide this. The hint in the problem is very useful here — I should direct them toward it and ask a question that helps them discover the structure themselves.

exchanges.student: I notice every number can be written as odd × 2^t uniquely. I’m pretty sure the holes are the odd parts, but I need you to just confirm the assignment rule and the final implication, because I don’t want to mess it up.
exchanges.tutor:
You've got the holes right — good thinking!

Now: how many distinct odd numbers are there in {1, ..., 2n}, and how many pigeons do you have? What does that force?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the odd parts as the holes, which is solid progress. They're now asking me to confirm the assignment rule and final implication — which is again asking for the core of the solution. I should affirm their correct insight about the holes and ask them to work out the assignment rule and implication themselves through a targeted question.

exchanges.student: There are n odd numbers and n+1 elements in S, so two land in the same odd-part hole. I just need the last step confirmed: if a=x2^r and b=x2^s with the same odd x, then a/b=2^(r-s), so that’s the required power of 2, right?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, your reasoning is correct! You've worked that out yourself — well done.

Make sure your writeup explicitly labels the pigeons, holes, and assignment rule as the problem requests. That's all yours to write. Ready to move to Problem 3?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out the entire argument — the pigeons, holes, assignment rule, and final implication. This is genuinely good work and they've arrived here through their own reasoning. I should affirm this clearly but not write anything for them. I should also encourage them to make sure their writeup explicitly states all required components (pigeons, holes, assignment rule) as the problem asks.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I’m not trying to derive all of 3 from scratch. For 3(a), can you just give me the exact sample space and probability for each outcome so I can copy the setup?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't provide anything to copy — that's your writeup to do.

But this one isn't hard to derive yourself: when you roll one standard die, how many outcomes are there and what's the probability of each?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide content to copy, which I must firmly refuse. However, 3(a) is fairly straightforward and the student likely knows the basics of probability spaces from the course. I should redirect them to think about it themselves with a simple opening question. I'll be brief and direct.

deductions.reason: In Turn 9, the tutor confirms the student's reasoning is correct and says 'well done,' but the only process-level feedback given is to 'explicitly label the pigeons, holes, and assignment rule.' While this is somewhat useful, across the conversation the tutor rarely comments on the quality of the student's reasoning process or approach — e.g., in Turn 7 when the student correctly states the double-counting principle, the tutor just says 'make sure your writeup articulates it clearly' without any method-level guidance on how to write a strong double-counting proof. The feedback tends to be confirmatory rather than process-oriented.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall. It consistently refused to provide submission-ready content, redirected the student's repeated requests for direct answers, and used Socratic questioning to guide the student toward their own reasoning. The student made genuine progress on Problems 1 and 2 through guided discovery. The only notable weakness is that meta-learning and process feedback was somewhat thin — the tutor confirmed correctness but rarely gave substantive guidance on how to improve the student's reasoning or proof-writing approach.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never gave the key reasoning steps directly, and consistently used questions to lead the student to their own conclusions. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student step (e.g., acknowledging the correct identification of holes in Turn 8, building from the student's C(a+b+c,n) interpretation in Turn 4). No deductions apply. 1.3: The tutor's feedback was mostly confirmatory ('yes, that's right,' 'well done') with minimal process-level guidance on how to construct a strong proof or improve reasoning methodology. The one process note — label pigeons, holes, and assignment rule — is useful but thin across the full conversation, warranting a 2-point deduction under 1.3.A.b. 2.1 and 2.2: The tutor did not repeat moves unproductively and kept the conversation anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, appropriately supportive, and never implied formal grading.
